perturbation.original_text: In consideration of the Disclosing Party providing Confidential Information, at its discretion , to the Receiving Party, the Receiving Party shall: Keep the Confidential Information secret and confidential; Neither disclose nor permit the disclosure of any Confidential Information to any person, except for disclosure to Authorised Persons in accordance with clause 3, or to a court or other public body in accordance with clause 4; Not use the Confidential Infor mation for any purpose, whether commercial or non commercial, other than the Permitted Purpose; [Make only such limited number of copies of the Confidential Information as are required for the Permitted Purpose, and provide those copies only to Authorised Persons];] and Take proper and all reasonable measures to ensure the confidentiality of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of the Disclosing Party providing Confidential Information, at its discretion, to the Receiving Party, the Receiving Party shall: Keep the Confidential Information confidential; Neither disclose nor permit the disclosure of any Confidential Information to any person, except to a court or other public body in accordance with clause 4; Not use the Confidential Information for any purpose, whether commercial or non-commercial, other than the Permitted Purpose; and Take proper and all reasonable measures to ensure the confidentiality of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the authorization of disclosure to 'Authorised Persons in accordance with clause 3', the Receiving Party is now obligated to not disclose Confidential Information to any person (except to a court or other public body). This contradicts Clause 3, which explicitly defines and allows disclosure to 'Authorised Persons' under certain conditions. This change creates uncertainty because it is unclear whether clause 3 can be followed or is now voided.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party may disclose the Confidential Information to those of its officers, employees (together, ‘Authorised Persons’) who : reasonably need to receive the Confidential Information to enable the Receiving Party to achieve the Permitted Purpose; have been informed by the Receiving Party (a) of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information and (b) that the Disclosing Party provided the Confidential Information to the Receiving Party subject to the provisions of a written confidentiality agreement; in the case of the Receiving Party’s officers and employees, have written confidentiality obligations to the Receiving P arty that (a) are no less onerous than the provisions of this Agreement and (b) apply to the Confidential Information, and who have been instructed to treat the Confidential Information as confidential; have been provided with a copy of this Agreement and have agreed with the Receiving Party in writing to comply with the obligations of the Receiving Party under this Agreement, and that agreement provides that the Disclosing Party will be entitled to enforce the agreement as a third -party beneficiary; a nd in the case of the Receiving Party’s solicitors, have confirmed that they will treat the Confidential Information as if it were the Receiving Party’s confidential information and therefore subject to the rules of the Law Society concerning client infor mation. The Receiving Party shall be responsible for taking reasonable action to ensure that its Authorised Persons comply with the Receiving Party’s obligations under this Agreement and shall be liable to the Disclosing Party for any breach of this Agree ment by such Authorised Persons.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows disclosure to Authorised Persons, while the changed text omits this exception, now only allowing disclosure to a court or public body. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which details the conditions under which the Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information to Authorised Persons. The omission creates a strong contradiction because it's no longer clear if the Receiving Party can follow clause 3, as it conflicts with the new text. Therefore, there is an in-text contradiction as the change narrows the disclosure, breaking compatibility with the contradicted section.

perturbation.original_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, the following words shall have the following meanings: ‘Information’ shall include information wheth er of a technical, commercial or any other nature whatsoever provided directly or indirectly by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party in oral or documentary form or by way of models, biological or chemical materials or other tangible form or by dem onstrations and whether before, on or after the date of this Agreement. ‘Confidential Information’ shall mean: in respect of Information provided in documentary or by way of a model or in other tangible form, Information which at the time of provision is marked or otherwise designated to show expressly or by necessary implication that it is imparted in confidence; and in respect of Information that is imparted orally, any information that the Disclosing Party or its representatives informed the Receiving Party at the time of disclosure was imparted in confidence; and in respect of Confidential Information imparted orally, any note or record of the disclosure and any evaluation materials prepared by the Receiving Party that incorporate any Confidential Information; and any copy of any of the foregoing; and the fact that discussions are taking place between the Receiving Party and the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, the following words shall have the following meanings: ‘Information’ shall include information whether of a technical, commercial or any other nature whatsoever provided directly or indirectly by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party in oral or documentary form or by way of demonstrations and whether before, on or after the date of this Agreement. ‘Confidential Information’ shall mean: in respect of Information provided in documentary form, Information which at the time of provision is marked or otherwise designated to show expressly or by necessary implication that it is imparted in confidence; and any copy of any of the foregoing; and the fact that discussions are taking place between the Receiving Party and the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'by way of a model or in other tangible form' and 'in respect of Information that is imparted orally, any information that the Disclosing Party or its representatives informed the Receiving Party at the time of disclosure was imparted in confidence; and in respect of Confidential Information imparted orally, any note or record of the disclosure and any evaluation materials prepared by the Receiving Party that incorporate any Confidential Information;' from the definition of 'Confidential Information', the agreement now implies that information shared via models, tangible forms, or orally are no longer considered confidential. This creates a contradiction because the rest of the agreement refers to 'Confidential Information' without this limitation, implying all forms of information are protected, making it ambiguous as to which types of information are to be protected.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Confidentiality obligations In consideration of the Disclosing Party providing Confidential Information, at its discretion , to the Receiving Party, the Receiving Party shall: Keep the Confidential Information secret and confidential; Neither disclose nor permit the disclosure of any Confidential Information to any person, except for disclosure to Authorised Persons in accordance with clause 3, or to a court or other public body in accordance with clause 4; Not use the Confidential Infor mation for any purpose, whether commercial or non commercial, other than the Permitted Purpose; [Make only such limited number of copies of the Confidential Information as are required for the Permitted Purpose, and provide those copies only to Authorised Persons];] and Take proper and all reasonable measures to ensure the confidentiality of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits key categories of 'Confidential Information' (information shared via models, tangible forms, or orally) from its definition. The contradicted text imposes confidentiality obligations on the Receiving Party for all 'Confidential Information' provided by the Disclosing Party. Since the changed text narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information', it now creates a contradiction because the contradicted section implies broader protection than is now defined, leading to ambiguity about which specific types of information the Receiving Party is obligated to keep confidential. This contradiction aligns with the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1 Subject to clause 6.2, the Receiving Party shall (a) at the Disclosing Party’s request, and al so (b) upon any termination of this Agreement: Return and provide to the Disclosing Party all documents and other materials that contain any of the Confidential Information, including all copies made by the Receiving Party representatives; Permanently del ete all electronic copies of Confidential Information from the Receiving Party’s computer systems; and Provide to the Disclosing Party a certificate, signed by an officer of the Receiving Party, confirming that the obligations referred to in clause 6.1 h as been met.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 Subject to clause 6.2, the Receiving Party shall (a) at the Disclosing Party’s request, and al so (b) upon any termination of this Agreement: Return and provide to the Disclosing Party all documents and other materials that contain any of the Confidential Information, including all copies made by the Receiving Party representatives; and Provide to the Disclosing Party a certificate, signed by an officer of the Receiving Party, confirming that the obligations referred to in clause 6.1 h as been met.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'Permanently delete all electronic copies of Confidential Information from the Receiving Party’s computer systems;', it now implies that the receiving party does not have to delete electronic copies of confidential information but must return physical copies and provide a certificate of compliance. This contradicts the spirit of confidentiality and creates an enforceability problem as the Disclosing Party has no verifiable way of knowing if electronic copies remain and are being used.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party Acknowledges and agrees that all property, including intellectual property, in Confidentia l Information disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party shall remain with and be vested in the Disclosing Party and/or the Disclosing Parties Clients.
perturbation.justification: The original text required the Receiving Party to permanently delete all electronic copies of Confidential Information. The changed text removes this obligation, meaning the Receiving Party no longer has to delete electronic copies. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that all intellectual property in Confidential Information disclosed to the Receiving Party remains vested in the Disclosing Party. By not deleting electronic copies, the Receiving Party could potentially retain and use the Disclosing Party's intellectual property, creating a strong in-text contradiction, and conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 8. This Agreement does not include, expressly or by implication, any representations, warranties or othe r obligations: To grant the Receiving Party any licence or rights other than as may be expressly stated In this Agreement; To require the Disclosing Party to disclose, co ntinue disclosing or update any Confidential Information;To require the Disclosing Party to negotiate or continue negotiating with the Receiving Party with respect to any further agreement and either party may withdraw from such negotiations at any time without liability; nor As to the accuracy, efficacy, complet eness, capabilities, safety or any other qualities Whatsoever of any information or materials provided under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. This Agreement does not include, expressly or by implication, any representations, warranties or othe r obligations: To grant the Receiving Party any licence or rights other than as may be expressly stated In this Agreement; To require the Disclosing Party to disclose, co ntinue disclosing or update any Confidential Information;To require the Disclosing Party to negotiate or continue negotiating with the Receiving Party with respect to any further agreement and either party may withdraw from such negotiations at any time without liability; nor As to the safety or any other qualities Whatsoever of any information or materials provided under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'accuracy, efficacy, completeness, capabilities' the Disclosing Party may now be held liable for inaccurate, inefficacious, incomplete, or incapable information. This creates uncertainty around the Disclosing Party’s responsibility for the provided information, as other clauses imply a duty of care in providing information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Confidentiality obligations In consideration of the Disclosing Party providing Confidential Information, at its discretion , to the Receiving Party, the Receiving Party shall: Keep the Confidential Information secret and confidential; Neither disclose nor permit the disclosure of any Confidential Information to any person, except for disclosure to Authorised Persons in accordance with clause 3, or to a court or other public body in accordance with clause 4; Not use the Confidential Infor mation for any purpose, whether commercial or non commercial, other than the Permitted Purpose; [Make only such limited number of copies of the Confidential Information as are required for the Permitted Purpose, and provide those copies only to Authorised Persons];] and Take proper and all reasonable measures to ensure the confidentiality of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly stated that the Disclosing Party had no obligations regarding the accuracy, efficacy, completeness, capabilities, safety, or any other qualities of provided information. The changed text removes 'accuracy, efficacy, completeness, capabilities' and only retains 'safety or any other qualities'. The explanation states that this change may now hold the Disclosing Party liable for inaccurate, inefficacious, incomplete, or incapable information. This creates a direct conflict with the Contradicted Text, which outlines the Receiving Party's confidentiality obligations in consideration of the Disclosing Party providing Confidential Information 'at its discretion', implying a duty of care. The omission thus creates uncertainty around the Disclosing Party’s responsibility, thus creating an in-text contradiction, leading to uncertainty, inconsistency, or unenforceable terms as outlined in the definition.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Disclosure to employees The Receiving Party may disclose the Confidential Information to those of its officers, employees (together, ‘Authorised Persons’) who : reasonably need to receive the Confidential Information to enable the Receiving Party to achieve the Permitted Purpose; have been informed by the Receiving Party (a) of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information and (b) that the Disclosing Party provided the Confidential Information to the Receiving Party subject to the provisions of a written confidentiality agreement; in the case of the Receiving Party’s officers and employees, have written confidentiality obligations to the Receiving P arty that (a) are no less onerous than the provisions of this Agreement and (b) apply to the Confidential Information, and who have been instructed to treat the Confidential Information as confidential; have been provided with a copy of this Agreement and have agreed with the Receiving Party in writing to comply with the obligations of the Receiving Party under this Agreement, and that agreement provides that the Disclosing Party will be entitled to enforce the agreement as a third -party beneficiary; a nd in the case of the Receiving Party’s solicitors, have confirmed that they will treat the Confidential Information as if it were the Receiving Party’s confidential information and therefore subject to the rules of the Law Society concerning client infor mation. The Receiving Party shall be responsible for taking reasonable action to ensure that its Authorised Persons comply with the Receiving Party’s obligations under this Agreement and shall be liable to the Disclosing Party for any breach of this Agree ment by such Authorised Persons.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Disclosure to employees The Receiving Party may disclose the Confidential Information to those of its officers, employees (together, ‘Authorised Persons’) who : reasonably need to receive the Confidential Information to enable the Receiving Party to achieve the Permitted Purpose; have been informed by the Receiving Party (a) of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information and (b) that the Disclosing Party provided the Confidential Information to the Receiving Party subject to the provisions of a written confidentiality agreement; in the case of the Receiving Party’s officers and employees, have written confidentiality obligations to the Receiving P arty that (a) are no less onerous than the provisions of this Agreement and (b) apply to the Confidential Information, and who have been instructed to treat the Confidential Information as confidential; a nd in the case of the Receiving Party’s solicitors, have confirmed that they will treat the Confidential Information as if it were the Receiving Party’s confidential information and therefore subject to the rules of the Law Society concerning client infor mation. The Receiving Party shall be responsible for taking reasonable action to ensure that its Authorised Persons comply with the Receiving Party’s obligations under this Agreement and shall be liable to the Disclosing Party for any breach of this Agree ment by such Authorised Persons.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'have been provided with a copy of this Agreement and have agreed with the Receiving Party in writing to comply with the obligations of the Receiving Party under this Agreement, and that agreement provides that the Disclosing Party will be entitled to enforce the agreement as a third -party beneficiary;', it means that 'Authorised Persons' are no longer required to agree in writing to comply with the NDA. This creates a contradiction, because the Receiving Party’s officers and employees have written confidentiality obligations to the Receiving Party that (a) are no less onerous than the provisions of this Agreement and (b) apply to the Confidential Information, and who have been instructed to treat the Confidential Information as confidential. This means that The Receiving Party shall be responsible for taking reasonable action to ensure that its Authorised Persons comply with the Receiving Party’s obligations under this Agreement and shall be liable to the Disclosing Party for any breach of this Agreement by such Authorised Persons. But they never agreed to the agreement, so how can they be liable?
perturbation.contradicted_text: Disclosure to employees The Receiving Party may disclose the Confidential Information to those of its officers, employees (together, ‘Authorised Persons’) who : reasonably need to receive the Confidential Information to enable the Receiving Party to achieve the Permitted Purpose; have been informed by the Receiving Party (a) of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information and (b) that the Disclosing Party provided the Confidential Information to the Receiving Party subject to the provisions of a written confidentiality agreement; in the case of the Receiving Party’s officers and employees, have written confidentiality obligations to the Receiving P arty that (a) are no less onerous than the provisions of this Agreement and (b) apply to the Confidential Information, and who have been instructed to treat the Confidential Information as confidential; a nd in the case of the Receiving Party’s solicitors, have confirmed that they will treat the Confidential Information as if it were the Receiving Party’s confidential information and therefore subject to the rules of the Law Society concerning client infor mation. The Receiving Party shall be responsible for taking reasonable action to ensure that its Authorised Persons comply with the Receiving Party’s obligations under this Agreement and shall be liable to the Disclosing Party for any breach of this Agree ment by such Authorised Persons.
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the requirement that 'Authorised Persons' agree in writing to comply with the obligations of the Receiving Party. The contradicted text/original text (which is the same in this case) states that 'The Receiving Party shall be responsible for taking reasonable action to ensure that its Authorised Persons comply with the Receiving Party’s obligations under this Agreement and shall be liable to the Disclosing Party for any breach of this Agreement by such Authorised Persons'. Because the Authorised persons no longer need to agree in writing, this creates an in-text contradiction, because without agreeing they cannot be held liable.

perturbation.original_text: 6.2 As an exception to its obligations under clause 6.1, the Receiving Party may retain one copy of the Confidential Information, in paper form, in the Receiving Party’s legal files for the purpose of ensuring compliance with the Receiving Party’s obligations under this Agreement. Following the date of any termination of this Agreement, or any return of Confidential information to the Disclosing Party (‘Final Date’), (a) the Receiving Party shall make no further use of the Confidential In formation, and (b) the Receiving Party’s obligations under this Agreement shall otherwise continue in force without limit of time.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.2 As an exception to its obligations under clause 6.1, the Receiving Party may retain one copy of the Confidential Information in the Receiving Party’s legal files for the purpose of ensuring compliance with the Receiving Party’s obligations under this Agreement. Following the date of any termination of this Agreement, or any return of Confidential information to the Disclosing Party (‘Final Date’), (a) the Receiving Party shall make no further use of the Confidential In formation, and (b) the Receiving Party’s obligations under this Agreement shall otherwise continue in force without limit of time.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'in paper form', the Receiving Party is no longer restricted to retaining only physical copies and is now able to retain electronic copies of the Confidential Information as well. This creates an ambiguity because under 6.1 the Receiving Party should return all documents and other materials that contain any of the Confidential Information including copies made. This would create a loophole for the Receiving Party not returning them and still claiming to be in compliance with the agreement
perturbation.contradicted_text: Subject to clause 6.2, the Receiving Party shall (a) at the Disclosing Party’s request, and al so (b) upon any termination of this Agreement: Return and provide to the Disclosing Party all documents and other materials that contain any of the Confidential Information, including all copies made by the Receiving Party representatives; and Provide to the Disclosing Party a certificate, signed by an officer of the Receiving Party, confirming that the obligations referred to in clause 6.1 h as been met.
perturbation.justification: The original text limited the retained copy of confidential information to 'in paper form'. The changed text removes this restriction, allowing for electronic copies to be retained. This directly contradicts the 'Contradicted Text' which states that the Receiving Party must return 'all documents and other materials that contain any of the Confidential Information, including all copies made by the Receiving Party representatives'. Since the Receiving Party is now allowed to keep electronic copies, but is obligated to return all copies, there is an in-text contradiction within the document based on the definition of 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction'.
